title: Add Straight and Curved Perimeter Parts
description: Add all exterior straight segments and arc lengths to obtain the total perimeter of a composite figure.
condition: Given a composite figure with multiple exterior straight sides and arc portions whose lengths are known or computed.
response: Sum all exterior linear and arc lengths to find the total perimeter.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a figure has straight sides totaling 14 and a quarter-circle arc of length $\frac{1}{4}(2\pi\cdot 3)$, then total perimeter is $14 + \frac{1}{4}(2\pi\cdot 3)$.

title: Arc Length of a Quarter Circle
description: Compute the arc length of a quarter circle as one-fourth of a full circumference.
condition: Given the radius $r$ of a quarter circle.
label: Principle / Model

title: Area Not Proportional to Diameter
description: Recognize that the area of a circle scales with the square of the diameter (or radius), not linearly.
condition: Comparing two circles where one diameter is a scale factor k of another.
response: Conclude that the area scales by k^2, not k.
label: Principle
example: If one diameter is 1/2 of another, then area scales by (1/2)^2 = 1/4.

title: Area of a Circle Formula
description: Use the formula for the area of a circle in terms of its radius.
condition: Given the radius $r$ of a circle.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Area of Fraction of a Circle
description: Find the area of a sector or fractional part of a circle by multiplying the full area by the fraction represented.
condition: Given a circle (or its radius) and a shaded region representing a known fraction (e.g., 1/4) of the circle.
response: Compute the full area πr^2 and multiply by the given fraction.
label: Principle
example: If r = 6 and the region is 1/4 of the circle, area = (1/4)π(6^2) = (1/4)(36π) = 9π.
notes: Requires recognizing the fraction represented by the shaded sector.

title: Area Using Circumference and Radius
description: Compute the area of a circle using A = (1/2)Cr when circumference and radius are known.
condition: Given the circumference C and radius r of a circle.
label: Principle
notes: Derived from rearranging A = πr^2 and C = 2πr.

title: Circumference Formula Using Diameter
description: Relate circumference to diameter using the formula C = πd.
condition: Given the diameter d of a circle.
label: Principle
notes: Derived from the definition of π as the ratio of circumference to diameter.

title: Circumference Formula Using Radius
description: Relate circumference to radius using the formula C = 2πr.
condition: Given the radius r of a circle.
label: Principle
notes: Equivalent to C = πd since d = 2r.

title: Compute Total Coverage from Rate
description: Use a coverage rate (area per gallon or per can) to compute total area covered.
condition: Given a rate such as 300 square feet per gallon and a number of gallons or cans.
response: Multiply rate by quantity to find total area covered.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 1 gallon covers 300 ft$^2$, then 5 gallons cover $5\times300 = 1500$ ft$^2$.

title: Distinguish Area vs. Circumference in Context
description: Determine whether a real-world situation requires area (surface) or circumference (distance around).
condition: Given a contextual description involving covering a surface or measuring around an edge.
response: Identify whether area or circumference is needed.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Painting a bullseye requires area because it covers a surface; measuring around a Ferris wheel requires circumference.

title: Evaluate Circumference with Numerical Substitution
description: Substitute a numerical value for radius or diameter into a circumference formula and compute the result.
condition: A known numerical value for r or d and a valid circumference formula.
response: Substitute the value and perform multiplication to calculate circumference.
label: Principle
notes: Includes multi-step computation: find diameter if needed, then multiply by π.

title: Pi as Circumference-to-Diameter Ratio
description: Recognize that $\pi$ is the constant of proportionality between circumference and diameter.
condition: Relating circumference and diameter of any circle.
label: Concept

title: Radius from Diameter
description: Determine the radius of a circle by halving the diameter.
condition: Given the diameter d of a circle.
label: Principle
notes: Fundamental relationship between diameter and radius.

title: Relationship Between Diameter and Radius
description: Express the relationship between diameter and radius as d = 2r or r = \frac{1}{2}d.
condition: Given either the radius r or diameter d of a circle.
response: Use d = 2r or r = \frac{1}{2}d to find the other measure.
label: Principle

title: Round Up to Whole Units in Context
description: When a context requires whole items (e.g., cans), round fractional results up to the next whole number.
condition: A calculated quantity of discrete items is not an integer and partial items cannot be purchased or used.
response: Round up to the next whole number.
label: Concept
example: If 1.9 cans are needed, purchase 2 cans.

title: Use 3.14 as an Approximation for π
description: Use 3.14 as a decimal approximation for π when calculating numerical values for circumference.
condition: A calculation involving π where a decimal approximation is needed.
response: Substitute 3.14 for π in the calculation.
label: Concept
notes: Using 3.14 is a conventional approximation rather than a derivable necessity.

title: Verify Equation Using Table Values
description: Substitute corresponding values from a table into a proposed equation to check if it holds.
condition: Given an equation and rows of numerical values for its variables.
response: Substitute values and evaluate both sides to determine if the equation is (approximately) true.
label: Principle / Model
